cards.front: What is the difference between a physical line and a logical line in Python?
cards.answer:
A physical line is a single line as seen in the editor (terminated by a newline). A logical line is a complete statement that Python parses — it may span multiple physical lines.
Python ignores certain physical newlines to form one complete logical line.
cards.note: This distinction is why multi-line constructs work at all — Python knows to keep reading.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: Certain physical newlines are ignored in order to form a complete logical line of code.

cards.front: What triggers implicit line continuation in Python?
cards.answer:
Opening an unmatched bracket: `[`, `(`, or `{`.
Python automatically continues the logical line until the matching closing bracket is found, across as many physical lines as needed.
cards.note: No backslash needed — the open bracket is the implicit signal. This applies to lists, tuples, sets, dicts, and function signatures.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: Certain physical newlines are ignored in order to form a complete logical line of code.

cards.front:
Trace the following: what is the value of `a` after this code?
```python
a = [1,
     2,
     3]
```
cards.answer:
`a` is `[1, 2, 3]`.
The three physical lines are treated as one logical line because the `[` opens an implicit continuation that closes at `]`.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings

cards.front: Can you add inline `#` comments to each physical line inside an implicitly continued expression?
cards.answer:
Yes. Comments can appear at the end of any physical line inside an open bracket.
The comment is ignored by Python and continuation proceeds normally.
cards.note: Useful for documenting each element of a long list or each argument of a function call.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: You may also add comments to the end of each physical line.

cards.front: What happens if you place the closing bracket `]` after a `#` comment on the same line?
cards.answer:
SyntaxError: unexpected EOF while parsing.
Everything after `#` on a line is a comment, so `]` becomes part of the comment and Python never sees the closing bracket — the list is never terminated.
cards.note: Always put the closing bracket on a new line when using inline comments.
cards.code:
# WRONG — causes SyntaxError:
a = [1, # first element
    2 #second element]
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: Note if you do use comments, you must close off the collection on a new line. i.e. the following will not work since the closing ] is actually part of the comment.

cards.front: Does implicit line continuation work for tuples and sets as well as lists?
cards.answer:
Yes. It works identically for all bracketed collections: `[]` lists, `()` tuples, `{}` sets, and `{}` dicts.
The rule is bracket-based, not collection-type-based.
cards.note: Also works for function definitions and calls — any open parenthesis starts implicit continuation.
cards.code:
# Tuple
a = (1, # first element
    2, #second element
    3,
    )

# Set
a = {1, # first element
    2,
    }
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: This works the same way for tuples, sets, and dictionaries.

cards.front:
Trace this dictionary definition — what is the value of `a`?
```python
a = {'key1': 'value1', #comment,
    'key2': #comment
    'value2' #comment
    }
```
cards.answer:
`a` is `{'key1': 'value1', 'key2': 'value2'}`.
The comment after `'key2':` is ignored; Python continues to the next physical line and finds `'value2'` as the value.
cards.note: You can even split a key-value pair across lines — the colon on one line, the value on the next.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings

cards.front: Can function definitions and calls use implicit line continuation across parameters?
cards.answer: Yes. The opening `(` of a function definition or call starts implicit continuation, so parameters or arguments can each appear on their own line with optional inline comments.
cards.code:
def my_func(a, #some comment
           b, c):
    print(a, b, c)

my_func(10, #comment
       20, #comment
       30)
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: We can also break up function arguments and parameters.

cards.front: What is explicit line continuation in Python and how do you trigger it?
cards.answer:
Placing a backslash `\` at the very end of a physical line (before the newline) tells Python the logical line continues on the next physical line.
Unlike implicit continuation, this works outside of brackets.
cards.note: The backslash must be the *last* character — even a trailing space after `\` will cause a SyntaxError.
cards.code:
if a > 5 \
    and b > 10 \
    and c > 20:
    print('yes!!')
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: You can use the \ character to explicitly create multi-line statements.

cards.front: Why is implicit continuation (open brackets) generally preferred over explicit continuation (backslash `\`)?
cards.answer:
Backslash continuation is fragile: a trailing space after `\` silently breaks the continuation with a SyntaxError.
Implicit continuation inside brackets has no such pitfall and is also more readable.
cards.note: PEP 8 recommends implicit continuation wherever possible.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings

cards.front: Does indentation matter for continuation lines in explicit line continuation with `\`?
cards.answer:
No. The indentation of continuation lines is entirely optional and has no effect on parsing.
Python only cares that the `\` is the last character on the line.
cards.note: This is different from block-level indentation, which is mandatory and meaningful.
cards.code:
if a > 5 \
    and b > 10 \
        and c > 20:
    print('yes!!')
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: The identation in continued-lines does not matter.

cards.front: How do you create a multi-line string in Python?
cards.answer: Use triple delimiters: either `'''...'''` or `"""..."""`. Everything between the opening and closing triple quotes — including actual newlines and spaces — is part of the string.
cards.note: Triple-double-quote style (`"""`) is the convention for docstrings.
cards.application: Docstrings for functions, classes, and modules.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: You can create multi-line strings by using triple delimiters (single or double quotes).

cards.front: Are physical newlines inside triple-quoted strings preserved or stripped?
cards.answer: Preserved exactly. Every character typed inside a triple-quoted string — newlines, spaces, tabs — is part of the string value.
cards.note: This is the key practical difference from single/double-quoted strings, which cannot span physical lines.
cards.code:
a = '''this is
a multi-line string'''
print(a)
# Output:
# this is
# a multi-line string
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: Note how the newline character we typed in the multi-line string was preserved. Any character you type is preserved.

cards.front: Can you mix escape sequences (e.g. `\n`) with literal newlines inside a triple-quoted string?
cards.answer:
Yes. Escape sequences and literal newlines are both valid and can coexist in the same triple-quoted string.
The escape `\n` produces an extra newline on top of any literal newline already present.
cards.note:
Be careful — `"""some items:

    1. item 1"""` produces a double blank line because `
` + the literal newline = two newlines.
cards.code:
a = """some items:\n
    1. item 1
    2. item 2"""
print(a)
# Output:
# some items:
#
#     1. item 1
#     2. item 2
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: You can also mix in escaped characters line any normal string.

cards.front: What is the indentation pitfall when using multi-line strings inside functions?
cards.answer:
Indentation inside a triple-quoted string is part of the string value. If the continuation lines are indented to match the function body, those spaces appear in the string.
To avoid leading whitespace on subsequent lines, break the indentation convention and start those lines at column 0.
cards.note: This is a common gotcha for beginners who expect the string to 'know' about surrounding indentation.
cards.code:
# Extra spaces in the string:
def my_func():
    a = '''a multi-line string
    that is actually indented in the second line'''
    return a
# prints: 'a multi-line string\n    that is actually indented...'

# No extra spaces:
def my_func():
    a = '''a multi-line string
that is not indented in the second line'''
    return a
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: Be careful if you indent your multi-line strings - the extra spaces are preserved!

cards.front: Are triple-quoted strings comments in Python?
cards.answer:
No. They are real string objects that exist at runtime and are part of the compiled bytecode.
They only act as comments when used as standalone expressions (the value is discarded), or as docstrings.
cards.note: Using triple-quoted strings as block comments wastes memory and can confuse tools. Prefer `#` for comments.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: Note that these multi-line strings are not comments - they are real strings and, unlike comments, are part of your compiled code.

cards.front: What is a docstring and how does it relate to triple-quoted strings?
cards.answer:
A docstring is a triple-quoted string placed as the first statement of a function, class, or module. Python stores it in the `__doc__` attribute.
It is the one accepted use of triple-quoted strings as documentation rather than data.
cards.note: Convention from PEP 257. Accessible at runtime via `help()` or `obj.__doc__`.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: They are however sometimes used to create comments, such as docstrings, that we will cover later in this course.

cards.front: Does Python have a native multi-line comment syntax?
cards.answer: No. Python has no block comment syntax. To comment multiple lines, you must prefix each physical line with `#`.
cards.note: Triple-quoted strings are sometimes misused as block comments but this is discouraged — they have runtime cost and mislead readers.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: Also, there are no multi-line comments in Python. You simply have to use a # on every line.

cards.front: What is the best practice for commenting code vs. writing multi-line strings in Python?
cards.answer: Use `#` for all comments. Use triple-quoted strings only when you actually need a multi-line string value, primarily for docstrings.
cards.note: This keeps intent clear: `#` = ignored explanation; triple-quoted string = runtime data or API documentation.
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
cards.source_text: In general, use # to comment your code, and use multi-line strings only when actually needed (like for docstrings).

cards.front: What is the difference between implicit and explicit line continuation in Python?
cards.answer:
Implicit: Python automatically continues when an opening bracket `[`, `(`, or `{` is unmatched — no special character needed.
Explicit: You manually signal continuation by placing `\` as the very last character on the line — required when no bracket is open.
cards.note: Implicit is safer (no trailing-space bug) and is the preferred style per PEP 8.
cards.code:
# Implicit (preferred)
a = [1,
     2,
     3]

# Explicit (when no brackets available)
if a > 5 \
    and b > 10:
    print('yes')
cards.source: Fred Baptiste Python · 01 Multi-Line Statements and Strings
